text: The surgeon performs lysis of adhesions for ninety minutes, carefully reducing the small bowel from the hernia sac and clearing the abdominal wall.
The surgeon incises the linea alba along the hernia margin, identifying the right rectus muscle. The retrorectus space is entered, revealing the anatomical structures beneath.
The surgeon develops the retrorectus space both superiorly and inferiorly, expanding the surgical field within the abdominal wall.
The epigastric vessels are visible on the posterior aspect of the rectus muscle as the surgeon continues lateral dissection towards the neurovascular bundle.
The surgeon makes a fascial incision approximately one centimeter medial to the neurovascular bundle, exposing the underlying transversus abdominis muscle.
The surgeon divides the transversus abdominis muscle fibers using scissor electrocautery, carefully dissecting laterally while ensuring the transversalis fascia remains intact.
The surgeon continues the myofascial release superiorly towards the diaphragm and inferiorly into the preperitoneal space within the pelvis, expanding the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects the cord structures in the pelvis, with the potential to concurrently repair an inguinal hernia if identified.
The surgeon develops a wide flap laterally, achieving extensive mobilization of the right posterior elements. The bare bloody muscle is retracted anteriorly, revealing the underlying structures.
Three robotic eight millimeter trocars are placed on the right side, just lateral to the semilunar line, in preparation for the placement of retromuscular mesh.
The surgeon measures the dissected half of the abdomen and cuts a 26-centimeter wide mesh for retromuscular placement, preparing to cover the area with the mesh.

Three tacking sutures are placed on the opposite abdominal wall to secure it during the second part of the dissection. The robotic arms are undocked, repositioned, and redocked with three new eight millimeter ports.

The surgeon begins dissection on the left side, joining the preperitoneal space inferiorly and entering the retrorectus space. The dissection plane is developed superiorly, revealing the anatomical layers.

The surgeon identifies the neurovascular bundles laterally and performs a transverse abdominis muscle release. A 12-inch, 2-0 reverse barb suture is used to close the posterior rectus sheath in a running fashion.
The surgeon closes the hernia defect using a running number one reverse barbed suture, securing the tissue edges together.

The surgeon sequentially tightens the sutures from each side with a low insufflation pressure of five millimeters of mercury, using quilting sutures to plicate the hernia sac and reduce seroma formation. A second barbed suture is then run in a similar manner.
The surgeon tightens the sutures, progressively closing the anterior midline from the outside to the inside, ensuring complete closure of the midline.
The surgeon unfurls the mesh, which adequately fits the retromuscular space. The mesh is not fixated, as it broadly overlaps and covers the closed midline defect.
The surgeon places the mesh over all eight-millimeter port sites, ensuring they are covered without the need for fascial closure.
